text:
JOURNAL OF CHILD AND ADOLESCENT PSYCHOPHARMACOLOGY
Volume 9, Number 4, 1999
Mary Ann Liebert, Inc.
Pp. 293-306
Case Report
Sertraline Treatment of 5 Children Diagnosed with
Selective Mutism: A Single-Case Research Trial
JOHN S. CARLSON, Ph.D., THOMAS R. KRATOCHWILL, Ph.D.,
and HUGH F. JOHNSTON, M.D.
ABSTRACT

In this single-case research study, we examined the efficacy and acceptability of sertraline
treatment in children diagnosed with selective mutism. We utilized a double-blind, placebo-
controlled trial of sertraline within a replicated multiple baseline/across participants re-
search design (n = 2; n = 3). Multiple methods of assessment involving across-setting in-
formants were completed repeatedly throughout the 16-week study. Follow-up data was
collected at 4 and 20 weeks poststudy. Assessment measures failed to demonstrate group
changes in mutism, anxiousness, and shyness. All individuals realized considerable im-
provement on some of these variables. Two of the five participants no longer met diagnos-
tic criteria for selective mutism following less than 10 weeks of 100 mg sertraline treatment.
A third participant was reported asymptomatic at 20 weeks poststudy. Treatment compli-
ance was high. Parent treatment acceptability ratings were highly favorable. Single-case re-
search methodology has considerable clinical utility in the medication treatment of selective
mutism. Frequent and repeated measurement across phases helped to highlight varying lev-
els of participant improvement across situationall> specific settings. Additional investigation
of the use of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors in treating selective mutism is warranted.
INTRODUCTION
Selective

mutism (SM) is a rare disorder of childhood characterized by a persistent lack of speech within
certain settings and/or specific situations, such as in school or when confronted with unfamiliar persons
at home. It is not due to a communication disorder or a lack of familiarity with the spoken language. It has
been speculated that it is a variant of social phobia (Black and Uhde 1995; Dummit et al. 1997; Steinhausen
and Juzi 1996). Deficits in academic performance and social communication are associated with this un-
derstudied and rare disorder (Ford et al. 1998; Leonard and Dow 1995). Prevalence within psychiatric re-
ferrals have been estimated at less than 1% (Carlson et al. 1994). Information has been obtained on the po-
tential benefits of psychosocial treatments for SM, particularly behavioral treatments such as stimulus fading
Departments of Psychology and Psychiatry, University of Wisconsin-Madison, Madison, Wisconsin.
Downloaded by UVA University Library of Amsterdam from www.liebertpub.com at 02/03/26. For personal use only.
CARLSON ET AL.

procedures (Kehle et al. 1997). Yet, significant issues remain, including the cost/time constraints of these
treatments, a paucity of documented generalization and maintenance of talking behavior, and improvements
in responsive speech but not in spontaneous speech. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) were
recently identified as a logical first-choice pharmacotherapeutic intervention for this population of children
(Leonard and Dow 1995).

Research on the use of medications to treat SM is in its infancy. Golwyn and Weinstock (1990) were the
first to report on the potential benefits of medication treatment. Their case involved the dopaminergic agent
amantadine and, subsequently, phenelzine in the treatment of a 7-year-old girl who had a 2-year history of
SM. Gradual improvement in verbal behavior was reported between the 6th and 16th week of phenelzine
treatment. After the 16th week and after school had reopened, the girl reportedly spoke freely to teachers,
children, and clinicians. The medication was tapered and withdrawn by week 24, and a 5-month follow-up
indicated continued verbal behavior.

The case study of Black and Uhde (1992) used fluoxetine in a 12-year-old girl who had never spoken a
word in school. Outpatient psychotherapy (at age 5-6), a program of behavioral therapy (at age 11), a
placebo trial of 3 weeks in length, and a medication trial of desipramine of 10 weeks in length had all been
tried unsuccessfully. Fluoxetine was then prescribed for a period of 1 month prior to the new school year.
Upon return to school, the girl spoke freely with adults and peers. Improvements in both social communi-
cation and social interactions were found after 7 months; however, the girl remained uncommunicative with
her physician. No information was provided about her status after medication was withdrawn.
Later, Black and Uhde (1994) utilized a double-blind placebo-controlled design to investigate the effi-

cacy of fluoxetine treatment of SM. Of 15 placebo nonresponders, six participants were assigned randomly
to be treated with fluoxetine at a dose of 0.6 mg/kg daily, and the other nine participants continued placebo
treatment for a period of 12 weeks. Two of nine rating scales completed by parents showed statistically sig-
nificant improvements in children receiving fluoxetine when compared to children treated with placebo.
The clinical significance of this was less clear.

Wright et al. (1995) examined the benefits of fluoxetine treatment for a 4-year-old girl within the con-
text of a multimodal treatment approach involving family therapy, behavioral therapy, play therapy, and
pharmacotherapy. Following 24 weeks of unsuccessful systemic and behavioral treatment, a trial of fluox-
etine was introduced into the multimodal approach. After 5 days of treatment, improvements in talking be-
havior appeared within familiar settings. After 20 days, talking behavior was reported improved in all set-
tings. Side effects reportedly were nonexistent.

Dummit et al. (1996) conducted a pilot study treating 21 children diagnosed with SM with fluoxetine for
9 weeks. Multiple measures were completed during baseline and treatment completion periods. End fluox-
etine dosages ranged from 10 to 60 mg, with a mean end dose of 28.1 mg. Improvements on rating mea-

sures of anxiety and mutism were reported for the majority of children. A reduction in anxiety and increased
speech in public settings, including school, were noted at the conclusion of the study. Younger children re-
portedly showed greater treatment gains compared to the older children. Behavioral disinhibition resulted
in discontinuation of fluoxetine in two participants.

Two additional case studies have been reported in the literature within the past 2 years. Guna-Dumitrescu
and Pelletier (1996) reported on the successful use of fluoxetine within a multimodal treatment of an 8-
year-old boy with SM, who also demonstrated substantial oppositional and aggressive behavior to the point
that he required psychiatric hospitalization. Lafferty and Constantino (1998) reported on the successful use
of fluvoxamine in the treatment of a 6-year-old girl with SM and comorbid obsessive compulsive disorder.
The child's speech quickly improved following less than 3 weeks on medication and after 2 days on 100
mg of fluvoxamine.

A variety of drugs are being used to treat SM despite a paucity of research in this area (Carlson et al.
1994). Approximately two-thirds (n = 199) of survey respondents (i.e., child and adolescent psychiatrists)
reported treating a case of SM, and a third of those reported using treatments involving psychotropic med-
ications (n = 71). Antidepressant medications (e.g., imipramine and fluoxetine) were most frequently en-
dorsed as being potentially beneficial for treatment.

Previous researchers indicated rationales for medication treatment of this disorder, involving the rela-
tionship between SM and anxiety disorders, the failure of nonmedication treatments to demonstrate signif-
Downloaded by UVA University Library of Amsterdam from www.liebertpub.com at 02/03/26. For personal use only.
SERTRALINE TREATMENT OF SELECTIVE MUTISM
icant changes in increasing speech, and the chronicity of a child's mute behavior with its relative impact

on academic and social functioning (e.g., Black and Uhde 1994; Dummit et al. 1996). The present study
adds to the current body of literature by investigating the effects of medication treatment both within and

between individuals via a multiple baseline/across participants design (i.e., double-blind and placebo-con-
trolled). The single-case approach is especially useful for rare and understudied disorders, such as selective
mutism, where considerable barriers prevent the completion of a traditional group research design (Kazdin
Single-case designs add experimental control to traditional clinical practice activities or case study in-

vestigations by including the following: (a) continuous or repeated assessment, (b) baseline assessment, and
(c) monitoring stability of performance across baseline and treatment conditions (Kazdin 1998). In addi-
tion, multiple baseline designs involve the coordination of simple phase changes at different points in time.

Phases are periods of time in which a condition such as placebo (A) or a specific dosage of medication (B)
is prescribed. In this design, the strength of a treatment agent is demonstrated by observing that behavior

change occurs following the onset or introduction of the intervention at different points in time. In essence,
the onset of the treatment phase at different times reduces threats to validity by ruling out the effects of ex-

traneous events on changes in behavior. We further enhanced the design utilized in this project by devel-
oping study conditions a priori and by randomizing participants to different phase lengths, specifically length
of the placebo phase (A). Thus, persons were subjected to treatment after varying duration of placebo, and

data were then compared both between (i.e., across children) and within the series (i.e., for the individual
participant). Clinically important is the fact that these designs insure that all children will receive the treat-
ment condition, yet at different points in time. In turn, reversing behavior as part of an ABAB design (i.e.,

a return to baseline) make it undesirable within clinical situations. Similar to research algorithms (Vitello
and Jensen 1997), single-case designs which utilize double-blind, placebo-controlled conditions add objec-
tivity and evaluate methods to clinical practice and they may help to more actively involve parents in mak-

ing data-based treatment decisions. A multiple baseline design allows for modest interpretations regarding
treatment generalization predominantly through visual analysis of interindividual and intraindividual com-
parisons (Kazdin 1998).
METHODS
Participants
A participant recruitment announcement was sent to approximately 300 school psychologists who were
members of the Wisconsin School Psychology Association. This letter provided diagnostic information on

SM (American Psychiatric Association 1994) and a parent flier regarding study participation. These re-
cruitment efforts identified 25 children who exhibited behavioral symptoms consistent with a diagnosis of
SM. Full prebaseline assessment materials were obtained for 12 children. These materials included a 19-
page SM survey (Ford et al. 1998) that thoroughly questioned parents about the diagnostic criteria for SM
as outlined in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual, 4th ed. (DSM-IV; American Psychiatric Association
1994). In addition, parents rated children on the Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL; Achenbach 1991) and

on mutism, shyness, and anxiety behavioral rating scales used in previous medication studies with this pop-
ulation of children (Black and Uhde 1994). All 12 children met criteria for SM based on parents' responses.
Of the 12 cases in which materials were returned, six parents and their children accepted invitations to par-
ticipate, and two children were excluded because treatment slots were already taken. A coexisting medical
condition excluded one of the six children. Informal discussions with parents or professionals associated
with the other 13 nonparticipating children most often indicated parental discomfort with medication treat-
ment, parental perceptions of appropriate academic, social, or family functioning warranting little or no in-
tervention, or problems pertaining to transportation to and from the clinic as prohibitive or leading to dis-
interest in the project. A description of the five subjects based on information from the initial screening is
provided in Table 1.

All assessment and treatment services were provided on an outpatient basis at no charge to participants.
The project lasted 16 weeks and required biweekly visits (i.e., once every 14 days) to the clinic. Follow-

up ratings from patients were gathered via mailings a month after completion of the project and 20 weeks

poststudy or 2 months into the following school year. Participants and their families traveled to the clinic
from distances up to 150 miles. The study, including consent procedures, was approved by the University
of Wisconsin Hospital and Clinics' Institutional Review Board.
Inclusion/diagnostic/exclusion criteria
Invitations to participate in the project were contingent upon information obtained from screening mea-

sures, including meeting diagnostic criteria for SM. Diagnostic issues pertaining to ethnicity, immigration,
or primary spoken language were not a concern with our participants. Adding to the reliability of a diag-
nosis was the fact that three of the participants had a prior primary diagnosis of SM from another clinician.
Moreover, the study psychiatrist ascertained a primary diagnosis of SM for all five participants based on

an extensive psychosocial interview conducted with the parents of the participants during the first clinic
visit.
Inclusion criteria were age of 5-17 years, a 1-year history of failed psychosocial treatment if less than 6

years of age (to insure that mutism was not limited to the first year of formal schooling), and a DSM-IV
diagnosis of SM. Exclusion criteria included the presence of a comorbid primary psychiatric disorder other
than SM, a history of medication treatment for SM, the current use of psychotropic medications (e.g.,
imipramine for enuresis), current involvement in psychotherapy, medical contraindications (e.g., high/low
blood pressure, seizures, history of head trauma), illicit substance use, pregnancy, and significant psy-
chosocial stress such as an impending move or divorce.
Independent variables
Independent variables included no medication, placebo, and active medication. Five treatment schedules
were established for the 16-week study period. All participants underwent a 2-week no medication assess-

ment phase. Subjects were assigned randomly to one of five treatment schedules that varied by length of
the placebo phase (2, 3, 4, 5, or 6 weeks). Thus, all participants had a 2-week no-medication phase, at least

a 2-week placebo-controlled phase, and a medication phase lasting between 8 and 12 weeks, dependent on
the randomly determined placebo phase length. Children
1 and 2 were the first to consent/assent. Children

1 and 2 were assigned randomly to a placebo phase length (i.e., 2 and 4 weeks of placebo, respectively).
Approximately 7 weeks later, consent/assent was obtained for children 3, 4, and 5, who were then each ran-
domly assigned to the three remaining conditions (i.e., 3, 5, and 6 weeks of placebo, respectively). There
Downloaded by UVA University Library of Amsterdam from www.liebertpub.com at 02/03/26. For personal use only.
SERTRALINE TREATMENT OF SELECTIVE MUTISM

fore, a multiple baseline involving two participants was undertaken and a replication of this design involving
three participants was completed 2 months later (Fig. 1).
Medication was administered in capsule form. Placebo and active medication were indistinguishable. Par-

ents, teachers, other school personnel, and treating psychiatrists were unaware of the timing of the med-
ication trial, including the type of research design (i.e., AB or ABAB) being used in the study. Supporting
the double-blind nature of this investigation was that poststudy interviews indicated parent, clinician, and
teacher perceptions of the logistics of the study design to be inaccurate. Dosages were dispensed in 2-week
intervals via 7-day pill boxes. Parents were instructed to give the medication in the early a.m. or in the
evening if the morning dose was missed. Compliance was carefully assessed at each visit and found to be

greater than 95% across the baseline and treatment phases. Unsolicited parent reports indicated that the dou-
ble-blinded conditions added strongly to the integrity of the treatment condition.
Dependent variables

A lack of standardized scales pertaining to mutism and a paucity of information regarding medication
treatment effects with this population led to the use of multiple methods of assessment, some of which lack
psychometric support. Dependent variables included behavioral rating scales of mutism, anxiety, and shy-
ness; school and community-based behavioral observations; an analogue assessment procedure completed
within the clinic; a parent-completed side effect ratings scale; and a treatment acceptability form completed
by parents at the conclusion of the research project. The majority of dependent measures used in this study
were similar to previous studies (e.g., Black and Uhde 1994; Dummit et al. 1996), with the exception of
the analogue measure, observational recording forms, and treatment acceptability rating form. Multiple-
blinded informants, including parents, teachers, and psychiatrists, completed these measures. We hypothe-
sized that mutism, anxiousness, and shyness as measured by ratings, observations and the analogue as-

FIG. 1.
Parent Goal Attainment Scale ratings across participants (target behavior: speaking).
Downloaded by UVA University Library of Amsterdam from www.liebertpub.com at 02/03/26. For personal use only.
CARLSON ET AL.

sessment measure would improve following onset of the sertraline treatment phase. In addition, we hy-
pothesized that parents would rate sertraline treatment as being an acceptable intervention strategy.
Treatment efficacy measures. Goal attainment scaling (GAS) is a method for allowing parents or others

to quantify the progress made on a specific target behavior to facilitate monitoring effects of interventions.
Reliability studies on the GAS ratings have revealed high interrater reliability scores (Kiresuk et al. 1994).
GAS ratings were completed weekly by parents. The scale consisted of 5 points, ranging from substantially
worse levels of speech (

— 2) to substantially improved levels of speech (+2). The target behavior for all
children was speaking. A score of 0 indicated the baseline level of the child's behavior (i.e., the child fails
to speak in a number of situations). During the initial clinic visit, parents indicated that a rating of +1 would
be indicative of the effectiveness of medication as all reported observing a lack of improvement or wors-
ening symptoms over the past few years despite psychosocial interventions.

Teachers (5 X per week) and parents (2 X per week) collected observational data on speech. Event record-
ing of words spoken (responsive and spontaneous) over a 30-min time period was utilized in the school set-
ting and in a community setting in which the parent identified the child as being mute (e.g., while shop-
ping at the grocery or retail store). Observers completed these recordings via the use of a log book indicating
the week, day, setting, and the number of words spoken (spontaneous versus responsive).
We developed an analogue measure to assess participant's behavior when faced with the expectation for

speech within the clinic setting. The analogue condition was a response cost program involving a loss of money
based on the distance the child needed to be away from the clinician before he/she could speak the "respon-
sive" phrase of "my dog has fleas" to his/her parents in a tone and volume similar to that used when speak-
ing within the home setting. Specifically, children gave up a nickel for every foot they walked away from the
clinician. The distance (feet) from the clinician at which the child spoke was recorded during each clinic ses-
sion. The psychometric characteristics of this analogue assessment method have not been determined.

Numerous behavioral ratings scales were completed to gather information on both positive and negative
effects of treatment. The 113-item Behavior Problems Scale of the Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL; Achen-
bach 1991) was administered at pre-, post-, and follow-up phases. The CBCL is a well-researched behav-
ior rating scale for children and has adequate to excellent psychometric characteristics.

Adapted Clinical Global Impressions (CGI) ratings (National Institute of Mental Health 1985) were ad-
ministered across all phases. These measures are similar to the instruments used within an investigation of
the efficacy of fluoxetine (Black and Uhde 1994). While the CGI is simple and easy to use, its psycho-
metric characteristics are unknown, and they have been found to vary across clinical populations, physi-
cians, and medications (Aman 1993). Parent, teacher, and resident psychiatrist ratings were gathered in ar-

eas of mutism, anxiety, and shyness. Severity and change ratings were obtained. Parent, Teacher, and
Resident Psychiatrist CGI severity ratings (mutism, anxiety, shyness) were completed on a 5-point scale
(1
= absent; 5 = severe). Parent and Resident Psychiatrist CGI change scales (mutism, anxiety, shyness)
were rated according to a 7-point scale (1
= markedly improved; 4 = no change; 7 = markedly worse).
Teacher CGI change scales (mutism, anxiety, shyness) were rated on a 5-point scale (1
= markedly im-
proved; 3 = no change; 5 = markedly worse). Parent ratings of anxiety and shyness severity were broken
down situationally to include home, school, and community ratings.

Abbreviated versions of the modified Parent Questionnaire (PQ; Gittleman 1985a) and the Teacher Rat-
ing Scale (TRS; Gittleman 1985b) assessed a number of behaviors related to SM (Black and Uhde 1994),
including behaviors associated with anxiety, social anxiety, and mood. Total scale scores and subscale scores

were computed. Whereas the modified PQ and TRS has been used in previous psychopharmacological re-
search, the psychometric characteristics of the abbreviated versions are unknown.

Finally, side effects were monitored in relation to treatment effects. Parents completed a 34-item rating
scale on side effects commonly associated with SSRI treatment (Black and Uhde 1994). These ratings were
obtained every 2 weeks during the clinic visit. The psychometric characteristics of this measure are un-
known.

Treatment acceptability measures. The Treatment Evaluation Questionnaire-Parent Form (TEQ-P; Kelly
et al. 1989) was completed at the conclusion of the treatment phase and examined issues of treatment ac-
Downloaded by UVA University Library of Amsterdam from www.liebertpub.com at 02/03/26. For personal use only.
SERTRALINE TREATMENT OF SELECTIVE MUTISM

ceptability, appropriateness, and effectiveness. Parents completed this measure after receiving information
about the results of the dependent measures and a review of the research design used in the project. Thus,
parents had reviewed the data on their children's mutism, anxiousness, and shyness during the baseline and

treatment phases prior to completing this measure. The TEQ-P items were adapted from the Treatment Eval-
uation Interview and have been found to differentiate acceptable from unacceptable treatments (Kazdin
1980). Examples of items include "this was an acceptable intervention for my child's problem behavior"
and "the intervention was a good way to handle my child's behavior problem."
RESULTS
Treatment efficacy

Results of Parent GAS ratings are illustrated in Fig. 1. Visual analysis indicated replication of improve-
ment in ratings across four of the five participants upon onset of the randomly determined medication treat-

ment phase. The fifth participant (child 3) demonstrated improved parental ratings on this measure five
weeks after treatment onset. While we were initially concerned about improved parent ratings for child 5

during the baseline phase, the return to prebaseline levels during weeks 6-8 resulted in our decision to go
ahead with the implementation of the treatment phase. It is also noted that, at week 16, all Parent GAS rat-
ings were at or above the predetermined goals of +1. To illustrate the magnitude of changes in GAS rat-
ings (in standard deviation units) from across placebo and treatment phases, effect size (ES) data were cal-
culated for each child (range = -1.12 to -2.05), and then ratings were pooled to calculate a group mean
(ES = -1.57). This means that, in standard deviation units, the mean GAS rating during the placebo phase
was 1.57 lower than the mean GAS ratings during the treatment phase.

Teacher and parent observations of spontaneous speech are provided in Fig. 2. Visual analysis of the col-
lected data indicated an increase in the frequency of spontaneous speech in previously mute situations dur-

ing the treatment phase for four children, three of which exhibited no speech during the baseline phase.
Similar speaking behavior within the school setting was observed by only the teachers of children 4 and 5,
albeit 4—7 weeks following the onset of the sertraline treatment phase. The emergence of speaking behav-
ior in the school setting for children 4 and 5 was supported by numerous school personnel other than the
observing teacher.
Results on the analogue assessment measure were quite varied. Interpretation of data from this measure
are limited. Improvement was observed during the baseline phase for children 1 and 4, whereas children 2

SERTRALINE TREATMENT OF SELECTIVE MUTISM

and 5 only earned nickels (i.e., said "my dog has fleas" at less than 40 feet from the clinician) at a point
that coincided with onset of the sertraline phase. Child 3 failed to speak within the clinic setting through-
out the duration of the project.

Parent ratings on the CBLC at the end of the study resulted in scores that fell below clinically at-risk
levels (by child, T scores = 54, 47, 34, 45, and 32). Pre- and posttest results were not statistically signifi-
cant, as the majority of subjects failed to demonstrate clinical levels of behavioral problems initially. Group
summaries of self-report and ratings (parent, teacher, and clinician) on areas of mood, anxiousness, shy-
ness, and mutism are provided in Table 2. Ratings across all measures demonstrated change in a positive
direction, with many measures changing only slightly across phases. Ranges of scores are provided to
demonstrate the variability across participants and individual responsivity to the sertraline treatment. Group
quantitative analyses, as indicated by the slight group improvements depicted in Table 2, failed to accu-
rately capture the clinical changes reported for individual children. In contrast, Table 3 summarizes par-
ents' reports of behavioral change or "firsts" following onset of the sertraline phase of the project. Indi-
vidual differences in the situationally specific nature of speaking behavior and behaviors associated with
SM (e.g., anxiety, mood lability) were best illustrated by these parental reports.
Data from the treatment acceptability rating forms were favorable. To the item "overall, the intervention

was beneficial for my child," all parents, except the parent of child 2, "strongly agreed." The parent of child
2 "slightly agreed" with the item, as they hoped their daughter would have generalized improvements of speak-
ing behavior to the school setting. Responses to other items on this measure are illustrated in Table 4.

Minimal side effects were reported by parents, children, and psychiatrists. No children were dropped
from the study or had medication altered due to the appearance of negative effects. The parents of child 5
did indicate problems with insomnia in their ratings occurring 2 weeks after onset of 100 mg of sertraline

treatment. However, later ratings did not reflect the existence of this symptom. Notably, another participant
of the same age and sex experienced this side effect, to a lesser degree, during the placebo phase.

Follow-up phase
At 4-week poststudy follow-up, all children were reported as continuing with 100 mg of sertraline treat-
ment. Continued improvements were evident via teacher and parent ratings of mutism, anxiety, and shy-
ness. At the 20-week follow-up, the following GAS ratings were reported (+1, +2, +1, +2, +1). Chil-
Downloaded by UVA University Library of Amsterdam from www.liebertpub.com at 02/03/26. For personal use only.
SERTRALINE TREATMENT OF SELECTIVE MUTISM

dren
1 and 3 were maintained on 100 mg of sertraline treatment. Both reportedly increased the number of
individuals and situations in which they now speak, however speech within the classroom remained nonex-
istent. A brief discontinuation of medication was reported for child 1. However, parents reinstated treat-
ment following the emergence of "obstinate and troubling" behaviors. At 20-week follow-up, children 2
and 4 were being treated with 50 mg of sertraline and reportedly no longer met the criteria for SM, as they
spoke normally in all situations and settings. Child 5 was no longer taking sertraline. Upon discontinuation
from medication, she reportedly demonstrated increased levels of anxiousness, but maintained the gains she
made in speaking. She reportedly exhibits diminished speech (e.g., quiet voice) only during "show and tell"
and large group discussions within her regular classroom, and her parents no longer report her to meet the
criteria for SM.
DISCUSSION
Treatment efficacy

Improvements in talking were observed within home and community situations during the sertraline
treatment phase. However, the results (i.e., talking) did not consistently correspond to the randomly de-
termined onset of the sertraline phase across all children, which is needed to demonstrate the internal va-
lidity of a multiple baseline design. Visual analysis of graphically presented data indicated that changes
in parent GAS ratings were replicated across four of the five participants. During the 16-week study, im-
provements in mutism behavior generalized from previously mute community-based settings to the school
setting for two of the five participants as evident from parent and teacher behavioral observations. Post-
study diagnostic interviews failed to yield a diagnosis of SM for these two children. Additionally, a third
child was reported by parents to no longer meet criteria for selective mutism when assessed at 20 weeks
poststudy (i.e., 50 mg sertraline), as she now spoke within the school and other settings where she was
previously mute. Consistent with previous investigations, parent global ratings and general impressions
captured emerging treatment effects across participants (Black and Uhde 1994) and, therefore, must be
interpreted with considerable caution. Effect size data from global ratings of change (i.e., parent GAS
ratings) were calculated and are similar to results from a study examining effect size data (i.e., median =

— 1.635) using best-evidence synthesis techniques to evaluate psychosocial intervention studies (Pionek
et al. 1996). The average effect size for parent GAS ratings (i.e., a global measure of mutism change)
across all participants in this research study was —1.57 (median = —1.73). This finding is indicative of
the substantial changes that occurred between the randomly determined baseline and treatment phases.
Considering factors pertaining to the time, money, and resource allocation associated with psychosocial
techniques, the clinically significant changes associated with the onset of sertraline treatment in our study
supports additional research in this area.

Improvements in mutism functioning as indicated by parent GAS ratings demonstrated a rapid onset
(i.e., within days of beginning treatment) of treatment effects for four of five participants. Such a pro-
gression of improvement also was discussed in three case studies, one involving fluoxetine (Wright et
al. 1995), fluvoxamine (Lafferty and Constantino 1998), and phenelzine (Golwyn and Weinstock 1990).
The role of disinhibitory side effects of SSRIs comes into question given the immediacy of behavioral
change as some parents reported their children to be more sociable and outgoing within the home set-
ting after and only after the start of sertraline treatment. Other studies more typically found 6-10 weeks
of treatment prior to observed behavioral changes (Black and Uhde 1992, 1994; Dummit et al. 1996).
This is the period of time that passed prior to speech emerging within the school setting for two of the
five participants in this project. Frequent and repeated measurement across phases helped to highlight
varying levels of individual progression or improvement across situations that may have gone unnoticed
within group studies. These included individual improvements in anxiousness, mood, and shyness. Pre-
vious studies have made reference to possible age-change relationships regarding medication or psy-
chosocial treatment effects (Black and Uhde 1994; Kehle et al. 1997). Interestingly, both participants
who became asymptomatic at the conclusion of this project were the youngest (i.e., 5 years of age at the
start of treatment).
Downloaded by UVA University Library of Amsterdam from www.liebertpub.com at 02/03/26. For personal use only.
CARLSON ET AL.
Treatment acceptability

Item endorsements on a measure of treatment acceptability were positive across all participants. It should
be noted that all parents chose to maintain their children on 100 mg of sertraline treatment at the end of
the study for an indefinite period of time. This level of commitment to a specific method of treatment was
unprecedented in the treatment histories of our participants, primarily due to the treatment resistant nature
of the mutism behavior. Acceptability ratings also provided some information about social validity issues
as parents agreed that the behavior of their "children and peers were more alike" at the completion of the

project. The level of acceptability ratings of sertraline treatment in this study parallels previously published
research on the social validity of psychosocial treatments (Elliott and Busse 1993).
Study limitations

The findings of this study are limited by a number of factors, including a lack of replication of behav-
ioral change immediately following sertraline onset across all participants, the nonconcurrent multiple base-
line methodology, a lack of a return to baseline phase to demonstrate the controlling nature of the sertra-
line treatment, and the inherent limited use of statistical methods with single-case data when studying limited
numbers of participants. Additional support for the efficacy of sertraline treatment via statistical testing (i.e.,
randomization tests) would have been obtainable with a concurrent multiple baseline across participants de-
sign. Finally, consistent, well-defined, and repeated observations within similar environments may help to
more descriptively explore the medication effects on SM.
Clinical implications

The multitude of data collected from this study indicated a "ladder-like" nature to SM, similar to hier-
archical structures developed for anxious conditions such as fear of flying. This paradigm is consistent with
behavioral techniques focusing on shaping and stimulus fading procedures to improve mutism behaviors.
For example, children with SM may need to go through a series of stages or steps prior to speaking in "the
most uncomfortable" of situations. In this study, the two children who were asymptomatic at the end of

treatment appeared to "climb their ladder" more quickly than the other three participants. In addition, they
retrospectively appeared to start at different "rungs," which we hypothesize led to a lack of findings demon-
strating group change across our participants. For example, both clinically improved participants first demon-
strated behavioral changes in "previously uncomfortable" social situations (i.e., increased eye contact and
other nonverbal behaviors). Next, they began speaking within the home to previously unspoken-to indi-
viduals. Third, they spoke in the community or in settings outside the home to individuals with whom they
would previously not speak. Fourth, they spoke to family members within the school situation. Fifth, they
spoke to a nonthreatening, non-family member within the school (i.e., a teacher and a friend who were pre-
viously unspoken to). Finally, this behavior then generalized to others, including classmates within the above
situations. Follow-up data indicated continued improvements consistent with a "ladder-like" analogy, and
one additional participant conversed at the "uppermost rung." Clarifying client's position on this ladder dur-

ing initial diagnostic sessions may prove helpful in individually monitoring treatment effects and more
strongly demonstrating treatment effects for individual clients as well as groups of participants. For exam-
ple, child 3, who failed to demonstrate change on the majority of dependent measures in this study, would

have shown significant strides or change on measures that more thoroughly examined nonverbal commu-
nicative behaviors. Thus, the child appeared to start at a lower "rung" when compared to other participants
in terms of communicative behavior.
CONCLUSION

Additional study of the pharmacological treatment of SM is clearly warranted. Substantial improvements
in some children along with limited samples allow for optimistic but guarded impressions of the efficacy
of medication treatment. The use of single-case design in this study revealed rather rapid onset of behav-
ioral change (i.e., within days) across four of the five participants. This finding is inconsistent with the ex-
Downloaded by UVA University Library of Amsterdam from www.liebertpub.com at 02/03/26. For personal use only.
SERTRALINE TREATMENT OF SELECTIVE MUTISM

pected antidepressant effects of SSRIs. In turn, questions arise regarding the actual mechanism of change
associated with the SSRI treatment of SM. Further clarity is needed within the psychopharmacological treat-

ment literature with respect to understanding possible dopaminergic effects (i.e., talkativeness or disinhibi-
tion) and serotonin effects (i.e., mood lability, decreased anxiety) of sertraline treatment. Additional focus
on combined behavioral/pharmacological treatments also is indicated. Consistent with a "ladder-like" anal-

ogy, it is hypothesized that combined treatments would allow children to more easily progress through their
hierarchically defined difficulties. Stimulus fading or shaping procedures are logical adjuncts to medication
treatment.
